problem_summary: 2014 MCM Problem B (discrete): build a model to rank the "best all-time college coach(es)," male or female, across a full century and across sports (basketball, football, baseball/softball, etc.), explicitly addressing whether the time-line horizon (coaching in 1913 vs 2013) matters, then present a top-5 list in each of 3 sports plus a 1-2 page non-technical Sports Illustrated article. This team built a "Coaches Ranking Model (CRM)": they reduce 12 raw career statistics to 3 principal components, construct a virtual "idealistic coach" vector, and rank real coaches by their (adjusted) cosine similarity to that ideal — first on a basketball case study (60 coaches: 30 male / 30 female), then generalize across eras, genders, sports, and countries.
models_used: Principal Component Analysis (PCA): 12 standardized career factors -> 3 components via correlation-matrix eigen-decomposition. Cumulative variance 76.97% (Z1 52.4%, Z2 16.1%, Z3 8.5%); interpreted as Z1=coach-ability, Z2=coach-influence-school, Z3=coach-influence-society (Google searches). Run in SPSS 17.0.

Cosine-similarity ranking against a synthetic 'idealistic coach': define a reference vector whose 12 components are the best (max) value of each factor across the pool, then rank each coach i by cos(theta) = (X_i . X_s)/(|X_i||X_s|). Higher cosine (smaller angle) = better rank. This is the core scoring engine (the 'CRM').

Adjusted Cosine Similarity: standard cosine is direction-only and ignores magnitude (their worked counterexample: (10,5) vs (2,1) gives cos=1 despite a huge skill gap). They mean-center / range-normalize each factor: x'_ij = (x_ij - E_j)/(X_jmax - X_jmin), pushing weak coaches to negative coordinates so the angle can span 0-180 deg and magnitude differences register.

Hierarchical cluster analysis (agglomerative, single-linkage, Euclidean/Minkowski q=2 distance): used as a PRE-FILTER, not the ranker. Splits the 60 coaches into 9 levels and keeps only the top clusters (24 coaches in the first 5 levels) so the cosine ranking runs on a smaller, stronger candidate set.

Linear regression for the time-line (era) correction: regress number of active NCAA coaches on year -> A(t)=0.734t - 1421.8; declare match difficulty proportional to active-coach count, D(t)=k*A(t). Integrate D(t) over each coach's career and average to get a per-coach difficulty coefficient C_i = (1/(T_end-T_start)) * integral of D(t) dt.

'Giant effect' capped difficulty multiplier: min-max normalize C_i, then C'_i = 1.5 if C_si>=0.5 else C_i+1 (multiplier bounded to [1,1.5]) so newer coaches get at most a 1.5x difficulty credit (rationale: younger coaches inherit knowledge from predecessors).

Social-influence era correction: divide Google-search counts by G = (s - s_min)/(s_max - s_min) + 1 (G in [1,2], s = start year) to down-weight the internet-era visibility advantage of recent coaches.

One-way ANOVA for gender effects: F = MSSA/MSSE, hypothesis test at alpha=0.05 on each factor to find which differ significantly by gender. Result: NCAA-tenure (p=0.002), wins (0.004 region), losses, games, win-rate (0.006), teams (0.002), Final Fours (0.008), conference championships (0.000) are significant; raw wins (0.381) and NCAA championships (0.301) are not.

Data Whitening (variance equalization) for gender fairness: for factors flagged by ANOVA, rescale the higher-variance group (female) to the lower-variance group (male) about its own mean: x'_fwi = E_fw + (x_fwi - E_fw)/sqrt(S_fw^2/S_mw^2). Demonstrated on 'games won' (female std 383.3 -> 333.9, matching male 334).
z-score standardization (x' = (x-E)/S) applied before PCA and before clustering to remove unit/scale effects.

'Factors pool' construction for cross-sport extension: a shared, extensible database of per-sport factors (basketball adds NCAA tenure/championships; football/baseball add ties, bowl wins/losses/ties, bowl win-rate); pick the sport-relevant subset and re-run the identical CRM pipeline.
key_techniques: Reference-point / 'ideal alternative' scoring: instead of inventing arbitrary weights, build a best-of-every-factor virtual entity and measure distance to it — a TOPSIS-flavored idea expressed through cosine similarity, which sidesteps the hardest judging trap (unjustified weights).

Repurposing a recommender-systems metric (item-based adjusted cosine similarity, cited from collaborative-filtering literature) for a ranking problem the metric was not designed for — a creative cross-domain transfer.

Diagnosing and fixing a metric's blind spot in-paper: they explicitly show plain cosine fails on magnitude, then derive the range-normalized 'adjusted' variant — demonstrating they understand WHY the tool works, not just that it works.
Cluster-then-rank two-stage pipeline: cheap clustering culls weak candidates before the expensive/precise similarity ranking, improving both tractability and defensibility.

Mechanistic era correction grounded in a real driver: rather than hand-waving 'old coaches had it easier,' they pin difficulty to a measurable trend (growth in active NCAA coaches), regress it, and integrate it over each coach's actual career span (continuous-time integral, a nice discrete-to-continuous move).
Statistically-gated correction: ANOVA decides WHICH factors get gender-adjusted, so they only whiten factors that are provably different — avoids over-correcting and looks rigorous to judges.
Variance-matching (data whitening) as a fairness equalizer between two populations — an uncommon, sophisticated choice versus naive mean-shifting.
Bounded sanity constraints driven by domain logic (difficulty multiplier capped at 1.5 via the 'giant effect') to keep corrections from dominating the base signal.

Built-in validation: sensitivity (compare pre/post-optimization rankings) AND robustness (inject input noise into top-15 coaches, re-rank, show top-5 unchanged) — turning 'our answer is stable' into evidence.
Construct validity check: the model's top names (Wooden, Summitt, Krzyzewski, Bear Bryant) match expert/media consensus, used as an external reasonableness test.
why_it_won: "It directly and visibly satisfies every clause of the prompt — both genders (ANOVA + whitening), the era question (regression-based difficulty + social-influence coefficients), generalization across sports (factors pool, with football/baseball/softball top-5s delivered), AND the country extension (China) which was beyond what was asked. Judges reward a model that answers the literal question and then goes further. Methodologically it makes a defensible weighting choice: the 'idealistic coach + adjusted cosine similarity' framing converts a subjective weighting nightmare into a geometric distance, and the team pre-empts the obvious objection (cosine ignores magnitude) by deriving the adjusted version on the spot. The pipeline is layered and self-consistent (PCA -> cluster pre-filter -> cosine rank -> era correction -> gender correction -> extension), with each stage justified before it is used and the SAME engine reused throughout, which reads as engineering discipline rather than a bag of tricks. It closes with genuine validation (sensitivity + noise-injection robustness showing the top-5 is stable) and a face-validity check against expert consensus. Finally the communication is strong: a real magazine-style article up front, a clear model-execution flowchart, consistent notation, and an honest strengths/weaknesses section — the whole package signals competence and trustworthiness to a judge skimming hundreds of papers."
section_highlights: "Front-loaded deliverable: the required Sports Illustrated article ('Looking for the best all time college coaches? See here!') is placed BEFORE the table of contents with FAQ-style subheads ('Why is this system accessible?', 'And why is this system reliable?') — a judge sees the payoff immediately. Two visual summaries up top: a 'Model Execution Flow' table and a 'Model Optimization' table that let a reader grasp the entire architecture in seconds. Every model section follows a disciplined template: Model overview -> Justification -> Formulation -> Results analysis, so the reader always knows where the assumptions, math, and findings live. Assumptions are tabulated with explicit justification columns (not a bare bullet list). Heavy, well-labeled use of figures (cosine-angle explanation, cluster dendrogram demo, difficulty-vs-generation line chart, variance-before/after bar charts) that carry the argument visually. Side-by-side before/after ranking tables at each optimization stage make the effect of every correction auditable. Honest, specific Strengths/Weaknesses subsection (admits the model degrades when narrowed to 1-2 factors and is unreliable for less-impressive coaches). Photos and concrete stat tables of the final top-5 in each sport make results tangible."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The 'idealistic coach' is the per-factor MAX (one component's best value taken independently of others) — an unattainable Pareto-corner reference; cosine to such an extreme vector can compress differences among strong real coaches and is sensitive to outliers in any single factor.

Dimension bookkeeping is inconsistent: PCA yields 3 components, yet ranking is still done on the full 12-D (sometimes called 11-D) raw vector — the PCA result is interpreted but never actually used to reduce the ranking space, so a major model stage does little downstream work.

Google search count as the entire proxy for 'social influence' is anachronistic and biased: it strongly favors recent/currently-famous coaches and is confounded by name commonality; the era 'social-influence coefficient' only partially patches a fundamentally noisy signal.

The era-difficulty argument equates 'more active coaches' with 'harder to win,' and sets the proportionality constant k=1 by fiat; the regression A(t)=0.734t-1421.8 is fit to a suspiciously clean near-perfect line and the difficulty cap of 1.5 is asserted, not derived.

Data whitening only equalizes VARIANCE between genders, leaving mean differences intact, and the paper notes female rankings still 'declined slightly' — so the fairness correction is incomplete and arguably just rescales noise.

Small, convenience sample (60 coaches, claimed from 'NCCAB/National Committee Association America Basketball' — a garbled/likely non-existent body) with no stated sampling frame; selection of which 60 coaches enter the pool heavily determines both the 'idealistic' max-vector and the final ranking.

Robustness test is weak: noise is added only to the top-15 and only checks that the top-5 order is preserved — it does not perturb model parameters (k, the 1.5 cap, cluster linkage) or test rank stability deeper in the list.

Numerous data/consistency errors and typos: ANOVA significance text contradicts its own table for 'wins', baseball and softball tables are both mislabeled 'Softball', 'idealistic coach' Google value (18.1k) is wildly inconsistent with the raw-data Google figures (millions), and 'ANOVA' is repeatedly misspelled 'AVOVA' — minor individually but they signal rushed QA.

Cross-sport/cross-country extensions (football, baseball, China top-5) are asserted with result tables but almost no shown computation or data, so their credibility rests entirely on the reader trusting the pipeline transferred cleanly.
Cluster pre-filtering with single-linkage is prone to chaining; using it to DISCARD coaches risks dropping a legitimately strong but stylistically different coach before they are ever scored.
reusable_patterns: The 'idealistic alternative + similarity-to-ideal' ranking template: for any 'rank/choose the best entity' problem, synthesize a best-in-class reference vector and score candidates by distance/similarity to it — a defensible, weight-free alternative to ad-hoc weighted sums (and a lighter cousin of TOPSIS).

Always interrogate your similarity/distance metric for a blind spot and patch it explicitly: show a 2-line counterexample where the naive metric fails, then introduce the corrected version. Judges love seeing the failure mode named.
Two-stage cluster-then-rank: use cheap unsupervised clustering to prune the candidate pool before applying a precise but costly ranking metric — scales the method and adds an extra justified step.

Ground every 'fairness/era' correction in a measurable driver and gate it statistically: regress the correction factor on a real covariate, and use ANOVA (or similar) to decide which variables actually need adjusting before adjusting them.
Variance-equalization (data whitening, x' = E + (x-E)/sqrt(var_ratio)) as a reusable tool to make two sub-populations comparable on a feature without destroying within-group structure.
Bound your correction coefficients with a domain-justified cap (here [1,1.5]) so an auxiliary adjustment can never overwhelm the primary signal — cite the real-world reason for the bound.

Integrate a time-varying coefficient over an entity's active lifespan ( (1/Δt)∫ f(t)dt ) to convert a per-year effect into a single fair per-entity number — a clean discrete-to-continuous modeling move for any longitudinal comparison.

Validate with BOTH sensitivity (does re-ranking after each model change move the leaders?) and robustness (inject input noise, confirm the headline result is stable) — and add a face-validity check against an external expert/consensus list.

Structure the report as: front-loaded lay-audience deliverable -> model-execution flowchart/table -> per-model [overview/justification/formulation/results] -> extensions -> stability analysis -> honest strengths/weaknesses. The repeated 4-part section template makes a long paper easy for a fast-skimming judge.

Build an extensible 'factors pool' so one core engine generalizes across variants (sports/countries) by swapping the active feature subset — present the generality as a designed feature, not an afterthought, and show at least one worked extension.
